source: CDC 2022 revised milestone checklists (Zubler et al., Pediatrics 2022) + AAP developmental surveillance guidelines (Lipkin & Macias, Pediatrics 2020) + founder question bank

universalRedFlags.text: Has your child lost any skill they could do before?
universalRedFlags.subtext: Stopped saying words? Stopped walking? Stopped using hands?
universalRedFlags.escalationCondition: Red if clear regression; Orange if unclear but suspected
universalRedFlags.notes: Asked every check-in regardless of age. Any confirmed skill regression is a red flag requiring immediate follow-up.

universalRedFlags.text: Does your child respond to loud sounds?
universalRedFlags.notes: Asked every check-in, 0-36m. Persistent failure to respond to loud sounds warrants hearing evaluation.

universalRedFlags.text: Does your child look at you when you talk or play?
universalRedFlags.escalationCondition: R1 initially, escalates to R2 if persistent
universalRedFlags.notes: Asked every check-in, 0-36m. Yellow initially; Orange if persistent. Action profile depends on developmental cluster context.

universalRedFlags.text: When you call your child's name, do they look?
universalRedFlags.notes: Emphasise 9-18m but still ask at all ages. Orange if persistent failure to respond to name beyond expected window.

universalRedFlags.text: Does your child point to show interest?
universalRedFlags.subtext: Pointing to share, not only to request.
universalRedFlags.escalationCondition: Orange if persistent absence of declarative pointing
universalRedFlags.notes: Emphasise 12-24m but still ask at all ages. Distinguishing declarative (to share) from imperative (to request) pointing is clinically important.

questions.text: When you pick up your baby or talk softly, does baby calm down?
questions.notes: Self-regulation with caregiver support; foundational social-emotional milestone.

questions.text: Does baby look at your face?
questions.notes: Visual fixation on faces is an early indicator of social engagement and vision adequacy.

questions.text: Does baby seem happy to see you when you come near?
questions.notes: Early social responsiveness; distinguishable from reflexive responses by 6-8 weeks.

questions.text: Does baby smile when you smile or talk?
questions.notes: Social smile is a key 2-month milestone; absence after 3 months warrants monitoring.

questions.text: Does baby make sounds other than crying?
questions.subtext: Coo, grunt.
questions.escalationCondition: R2 if persistent absence of non-cry vocalisation
questions.notes: Pre-linguistic vocalisation; absence beyond 3 months warrants language pathway follow-up.

questions.text: Does baby react to loud sounds?
questions.notes: Startle reflex and auditory orienting. Persistent absence suggests hearing impairment requiring evaluation.

questions.text: Does baby watch you as you move?
questions.notes: Visual tracking of moving people; indicates both vision adequacy and early social attention.

questions.text: Does baby look at a toy or object for a few seconds?
questions.notes: Object fixation; early visual attention and cognitive engagement.

questions.text: During tummy time, does baby hold head up?
questions.notes: Neck extension during prone positioning; foundational for subsequent motor milestones.

questions.text: Does baby move both arms and both legs?
questions.notes: Symmetrical limb movement; asymmetry may indicate neurological concern. RF tag because persistent asymmetry is a red flag.

questions.text: Does baby smile on their own to get your attention?
questions.notes: Spontaneous social smiling as communication initiation rather than just response.

questions.text: Does baby chuckle when you play?
questions.notes: Early laughter as social expression; typically emerges 3-4 months.

questions.text: Does baby look, move, or make sounds to keep your attention?
questions.notes: Intentional social bids to maintain caregiver engagement; early turn-taking precursor.

questions.text: Does baby make sounds like 'oooo' or 'aahh'?
questions.notes: Vowel-like cooing sounds; precursor to canonical babbling emerging later.

questions.text: When you talk, does baby make sounds back?
questions.notes: Proto-conversational turn-taking; critical foundation for language development.

questions.text: Does baby turn their head towards your voice?
questions.notes: Auditory localisation towards familiar voice; hearing and early receptive language indicator.

questions.text: If hungry, does baby open their mouth when seeing the breast or bottle?
questions.notes: Anticipatory feeding behaviour; early object recognition and associative learning.

questions.text: Does baby look at their hands with interest?
questions.notes: Hand regard; CDC 2022 lists at 2 months. Early self-discovery and visual-motor awareness. Retained in 3-5m band as confirmation check.

questions.text: Can baby hold a toy you place in their hand?
questions.notes: Palmar grasp becoming more voluntary; transitioning from reflexive to intentional grasping.

questions.text: Does baby use their arm to swing or reach at toys?
questions.notes: Visually-guided reaching; integration of visual tracking with motor output.

questions.text: Does baby bring their hands to their mouth?
questions.notes: Hand-to-mouth coordination; early oral exploration and proprioceptive development.

questions.text: On tummy, does baby push up on their elbows or forearms?
questions.notes: Prone prop on forearms; progression from 2-month head lift, precursor to sitting.

questions.text: When held, is baby's head steady?
questions.notes: Head control in supported sitting; hypotonia or persistent head lag warrants evaluation.

questions.text: Does baby know familiar people?
questions.subtext: Does baby relax or smile with familiar carers?
questions.notes: Differential social responding; emerging attachment to familiar caregivers.

questions.text: Does baby like to look at themselves in a mirror?
questions.notes: Mirror-directed behaviour; early self-other distinction and visual engagement.

questions.text: Does baby laugh?
questions.notes: Social laughter; emerging by 4 months, well-established by 6 months.

questions.text: Does baby take turns making sounds with you?
questions.notes: Vocal turn-taking; key precursor to conversational exchange and language development.

questions.text: Does baby blow raspberries?
questions.notes: Labial-lingual motor play; named in CDC 2022 6-month checklist. Oral motor exploration supporting later articulation.

questions.text: Does baby make squealing noises?
questions.notes: Vocal range expansion; high-pitched vocalisations indicate oral motor and respiratory control.

questions.text: Does baby put safe things in their mouth to explore?
questions.notes: Mouthing for sensory exploration; typical object exploration strategy at this age.

questions.text: Does baby reach and grab a toy they want?
questions.notes: Intentional reaching and grasping; visually-guided object acquisition with increasing accuracy.

questions.text: When full, does baby close their lips or turn away from food?
questions.notes: Hunger/satiety signalling; important for responsive feeding practices and self-regulation.

questions.text: Does baby roll from tummy to back?
questions.notes: Rolling is a key gross motor milestone; typically preceded by back-to-side rolling.

questions.text: On tummy, does baby push up with straight arms?
questions.notes: Extended arm push-up in prone; precursor to crawling and independent sitting.

questions.text: When sitting, does baby lean on their hands for support?
questions.notes: Prop-sitting; transitional stage between supported and independent sitting.

questions.text: Does baby sit with support or begin to sit without support?
questions.notes: Independent sitting; CDC 2022 6-month milestone (75th percentile). Foundational for subsequent motor development.

questions.text: Is baby sometimes shy or clingy with strangers?
questions.notes: Stranger anxiety; typical emergence 8-10 months, reflects secure attachment formation.

questions.text: Does baby show different facial expressions?
questions.subtext: For example: happy, sad, angry, or surprised.
questions.notes: Differentiated emotional expression; expanding affective repertoire.

questions.text: When you call baby's name, does baby look?
questions.notes: Name recognition is a key 9-month milestone. Persistent failure is a red flag for ASD and hearing loss.

questions.text: When you leave, does baby react?
questions.subtext: For example: look, reach, or cry.
questions.notes: Separation protest; expected with secure attachment development.

questions.text: Does baby smile or laugh during peek-a-boo?
questions.notes: Anticipatory pleasure in social games; also reflects early object permanence understanding.

questions.text: Does baby babble like 'mamama' or 'bababa'?
questions.notes: Canonical babbling with consonant-vowel reduplication; absence beyond 12 months is a language red flag.

questions.text: Does baby lift their arms to be picked up?
questions.notes: Gestural request for physical contact; early intentional communication.

questions.text: If you drop a toy, does baby look for it?
questions.notes: Early object permanence; understanding that objects continue to exist when out of sight.

questions.text: Does baby bang two things together?
questions.notes: Bimanual coordination; object relation play showing cause-and-effect understanding.

questions.text: Does baby get into a sitting position by themselves?
questions.notes: Independent sit-to-stand transition; requires trunk control and transitional movement.

questions.text: Does baby move objects from one hand to the other?
questions.notes: Hand-to-hand transfer; CDC 2022 lists at 6 months. Bimanual coordination and emerging hand preference exploration. Retained in 9-11m band as reinforcement check.

questions.text: Does baby use fingers to rake food towards themselves?
questions.notes: Raking grasp for self-feeding; typically emerges 6-7 months. Precursor to refined pincer grasp. CDC 2022 aligned.

questions.text: Can baby sit without support?
questions.escalationCondition: R2 if absent beyond expected window
questions.notes: Independent sitting; CDC 2022 lists at 6 months (75th percentile). Absence beyond 8 months warrants motor evaluation. Retained in 9-11m band as confirmation check.

questions.text: Does baby use finger and thumb to pick up small things?
questions.subtext: Like a piece of food between thumb and pointing finger.
questions.notes: Pincer grasp (finger-thumb opposition); CDC 2022 9-month milestone. Critical fine motor milestone absent from v0.1. Absence beyond 12 months warrants fine motor evaluation.

questions.text: Does your child play games with you, like pat-a-cake?
questions.notes: Imitative social games; joint attention, imitation, and social engagement.

questions.text: Does your child wave bye-bye?
questions.notes: Communicative gesture; typically emerges 9-12 months; part of gestural communication repertoire.

questions.text: Does your child call a parent mama, dada, or another special name?
questions.escalationCondition: R2 if persistent absence beyond 15 months
questions.notes: First words with specific referents; absence beyond 15 months is a language screening trigger.

questions.text: Does your child understand 'no'?
questions.subtext: Pauses or stops briefly.
questions.notes: Word comprehension for prohibitive language; early receptive language milestone.

questions.text: Can your child put a toy into a container?
questions.notes: Object-in-container; spatial reasoning, voluntary release, and goal-directed action.

questions.text: Does your child look for things you hide, like a toy under a cloth?
questions.notes: Object permanence with visible displacement; Piagetian Stage 4 object permanence.

questions.text: Does your child pull to stand?
questions.notes: Pull-to-stand; CDC 2022 lists at 9 months (75th percentile). Absence beyond 12 months warrants motor evaluation.

questions.text: Does your child walk while holding furniture (cruising)?
questions.escalationCondition: R1 initially, R2 if persistent absence
questions.notes: Lateral cruising; typically emerges 9-12 months as precursor to independent ambulation.

questions.text: Does your child copy other children while playing?
questions.notes: Peer imitation; social learning from other children, precursor to cooperative play.

questions.text: Does your child show you an object they like?
questions.notes: Declarative showing; joint attention gesture. Absence is a key ASD screening indicator.

questions.text: Does your child clap when excited?
questions.notes: Emotional expression through gesture; spontaneous clapping to share positive affect.

questions.text: Does your child hug a doll or toy?
questions.notes: Nurturing play with objects; early symbolic/pretend play precursor.

questions.text: Does your child show affection?
questions.subtext: For example: hugging, cuddling, or kissing.
questions.notes: Physical affection expression; attachment quality indicator.

questions.text: Does your child try to say 1 or 2 words besides mama and dada?
questions.notes: First words beyond caregiver names; CDC 15-month language milestone.

questions.text: If you name an object, does your child look at it?
questions.notes: Word-referent mapping; vocabulary comprehension and joint attention.

questions.text: If you say 'Give me the toy' and show your hand, does your child follow the instruction?
questions.escalationCondition: R2 if persistent non-compliance beyond 18 months
questions.notes: One-step command with gesture support; note this uses gesture cue unlike the 18m probe without gesture.

questions.text: Does your child point to ask for something or for help?
questions.notes: Imperative pointing; intentional gestural communication to request. Compare with declarative pointing in rf_05.

questions.text: Does your child use objects the right way?
questions.subtext: For example: using a toy phone to talk, a cup to drink, or a book to look at.
questions.notes: Functional object use; understanding objects' conventional purposes precedes symbolic play.

questions.text: Can your child stack 2 small objects?
questions.notes: Block stacking; fine motor precision, spatial reasoning, and release control.

questions.text: Does your child take a few steps alone?
questions.escalationCondition: R2 if beyond grace period (typically by 15 months)
questions.notes: Independent ambulation; CDC flags absence of independent walking by 18 months for evaluation.

questions.text: Does your child use their fingers to feed themselves some food?
questions.notes: Finger feeding; fine motor and self-feeding independence.

questions.text: Does your child move away from you to play but check that you are still close?
questions.notes: Secure base exploration; uses caregiver as safe haven while exploring environment.

questions.text: Does your child point to show you something interesting?
questions.notes: Declarative pointing; joint attention. Absence at 18 months is a significant ASD screening indicator.

questions.text: Does your child put their hands out for you to wash them?
questions.notes: Cooperative self-care participation; routine-based compliance and self-regulation.

questions.text: Does your child look at a few pages in a book with you?
questions.notes: Shared book-reading attention; joint attention, literacy precursor, and language enrichment.

questions.text: Does your child help you when dressing them?
questions.subtext: For example: pushing arm through a sleeve or lifting a foot.
questions.notes: Cooperative dressing; understanding routine sequences and body schema.

questions.text: Does your child try to say 3 or more words besides mama and dada?
questions.escalationCondition: R2 if fewer than 3 words at 18 months
questions.notes: 18-month language milestone; fewer than 6 words at 18m triggers M-CHAT-R/F or speech evaluation pathway.

questions.text: Can your child follow a one-step instruction without a gesture?
questions.escalationCondition: R2 if persistent non-compliance without gesture cue
questions.notes: Verbal instruction without gesture support; tests language comprehension independent of non-verbal cues.

questions.text: Does your child copy household chores?
questions.subtext: For example: trying to sweep.
questions.notes: Deferred imitation of adult activities; early pretend/role play and social learning.

questions.text: Does your child play with toys in a simple way?
questions.subtext: For example: pushing a toy car.
questions.notes: Functional toy play; conventional use of play objects before symbolic substitution emerges.

questions.text: Does your child walk without holding on?
questions.notes: Independent walking; CDC flags absence by 18 months for paediatric evaluation.

questions.text: Does your child scribble with a crayon or pen?
questions.notes: Mark-making with writing instruments; tool use and precursor to drawing and writing.

questions.text: Can your child drink from a cup without a lid (spills are okay)?
questions.notes: Open cup drinking; oral motor control and self-feeding independence.

questions.text: Does your child feed themselves with their fingers?
questions.notes: Independent finger feeding; self-feeding autonomy and fine motor control.

questions.text: Does your child try to use a spoon?
questions.notes: Utensil use attempts; tool use, wrist rotation, and self-feeding development.

questions.text: Can your child climb on and off a couch or chair without help?
questions.notes: Climbing on/off furniture; gross motor planning, strength, and postural control.

questions.text: Does your child notice when others are hurt or upset?
questions.notes: Empathic responding; early prosocial behaviour and emotion recognition.

questions.text: In a new situation, does your child look at your face to know how to react?
questions.notes: Social referencing; using caregiver's emotional expression to guide behaviour in novel situations.

questions.text: When you ask about a picture, does your child point to it in a book?
questions.notes: Picture pointing on request; vocabulary comprehension and shared reading engagement.

questions.text: Does your child put 2 words together?
questions.subtext: For example: 'more milk'.
questions.notes: Two-word combinations; absence by 24 months is a language referral threshold (CDC/AAP).

questions.text: If you ask, can your child point to 2 body parts?
questions.notes: Body part identification; vocabulary comprehension and body schema.

questions.text: Does your child use gestures beyond waving and pointing?
questions.subtext: For example: nodding or blowing a kiss.
questions.notes: Expanding gestural repertoire; communicative gesture diversity correlates with language development.

questions.text: Can your child hold a container and take the lid off?
questions.notes: Bimanual coordination with rotational movement; tool use and problem-solving.

questions.text: Does your child try out switches, knobs, or buttons on a toy?
questions.notes: Exploratory play with mechanical features; cause-and-effect understanding.

questions.text: Does your child play with 2 toys together?
questions.subtext: For example: using toy food with a plate.
questions.notes: Combinatorial play; relating objects according to their functional relationships.

questions.text: Can your child kick a ball?
questions.notes: Ball kicking; dynamic balance, weight shift, and lower extremity coordination.

questions.text: Can your child run?
questions.notes: Running gait; typically emerges by 18-20 months following established independent walking.

questions.text: Can your child walk up a few stairs (not climb them), with or without help?
questions.notes: Stair walking (mark-time pattern); progresses to alternating steps by 24-30 months.

questions.text: Does your child eat with a spoon?
questions.notes: Spoon use for self-feeding; utensil mastery and wrist supination control.

questions.text: Does your child put 2 words together?
questions.escalationCondition: R2 if absent at this age — prioritise screening
questions.notes: If missed at 21-23m band and confirmed absent now, prioritise immediate language screening/referral.

questions.text: Does your child say about 50 words?
questions.escalationCondition: R2 if vocabulary clearly below 50 words beyond grace
questions.notes: 50-word vocabulary is the 24-month benchmark; fewer than 50 words triggers speech-language evaluation.

questions.text: Does your child say 2 or more words together including an action word?
questions.subtext: For example: 'doggie run'.
questions.notes: Verb inclusion in two-word utterances; reflects understanding of agent-action relationships.

questions.text: Can your child name things in a book when you point to them?
questions.notes: Picture naming; expressive vocabulary and shared reading engagement.

questions.text: Does your child use words like I, me, or we?
questions.notes: Personal pronoun use; CDC 2022 places at 30 months (75th percentile). Self-referential language and emerging self-concept.

questions.text: Does your child play next to other children and sometimes play with them?
questions.notes: Parallel to associative play transition; typical 24m social play progression.

questions.text: Does your child use pretend play?
questions.subtext: For example: feeding a block to a doll as if it were food.
questions.notes: Symbolic substitution in play; requires corroboration. Absence of pretend play by 24m warrants monitoring.

questions.text: Can your child follow 2-step instructions?
questions.notes: Two-step sequential instruction; working memory and language comprehension.

questions.text: Does your child know at least one colour?
questions.subtext: For example: can they point to something red when asked?
questions.notes: Colour concept acquisition; CDC 2022 places colour knowledge at 36 months. normativeAge set to 33 months to allow appropriate grace window. Categorical perception and vocabulary development.

questions.text: Can your child twist things?
questions.subtext: For example: turning a knob or unscrewing a lid.
questions.notes: Forearm pronation/supination; rotational wrist movement refinement.

questions.text: Can your child take off some of their own clothes?
questions.subtext: For example: loose pants or an open jacket.
questions.notes: Partial undressing; self-care independence and fine motor skills.

questions.text: Can your child jump off the ground with both feet?
questions.notes: Two-foot takeoff jump; bilateral coordination and lower extremity power.

questions.text: Does your child turn book pages one at a time?
questions.notes: Single page turning; fine motor precision and page manipulation.

questions.text: Is your child showing signs of toilet readiness?
questions.subtext: For example: staying dry for about 2 hours, telling you when they need to go, or being able to pull their pants up and down.
questions.notes: Toilet training readiness indicators; physiological and cognitive readiness vary widely (18-36m range).

questions.text: Does your child calm down within 10 minutes after you leave, such as at a childcare drop-off?
questions.notes: Separation regulation; ability to self-soothe after caregiver departure. Persistent inability may reflect anxiety or attachment issues.

questions.text: Does your child notice other children and join them to play?
questions.escalationCondition: R1 initially, R2 if persistent social disinterest
questions.notes: Peer-directed social initiation; transition to cooperative play. Persistent disinterest may warrant social-emotional screening.

questions.text: Can your child have a back-and-forth conversation with at least 2 exchanges?
questions.notes: Conversational reciprocity; requires both expressive output and receptive monitoring of partner's turn.

questions.text: Does your child ask who, what, where, or why questions?
questions.notes: Wh-question use; emerging language form and cognitive curiosity.

questions.text: If you ask about a picture, can your child say what action is happening?
questions.subtext: For example: 'running' or 'eating'.
questions.notes: Action description from pictures; verb production and scene interpretation.

questions.text: Does your child use words to describe actions in daily life?
questions.subtext: For example: saying 'running' or 'eating' while doing those things.
questions.notes: Action verb use in context; connecting language to ongoing activities.

questions.text: Can your child say their first name when asked?
questions.notes: Self-identification by name; self-concept and name recognition.

questions.text: Do others understand your child's speech most of the time?
questions.escalationCondition: Orange if strangers cannot understand most of speech
questions.notes: Speech intelligibility; by 36m, 75% of speech should be intelligible to unfamiliar listeners.

questions.text: Can your child draw a circle when you show them how?
questions.notes: Imitative circle drawing; CDC 2022 places at 36 months (75th percentile). Visual-motor integration and copy-from-model skill.

questions.text: If you warn your child 'hot', do they avoid touching it?
questions.notes: Safety word compliance; language comprehension applied to safety situations.

questions.text: Can your child string items together?
questions.subtext: For example: large beads or macaroni.
questions.notes: Threading/stringing; bilateral coordination, pincer grasp, and eye-hand coordination.

questions.text: Can your child put on some of their own clothes?
questions.subtext: For example: loose pants or a jacket.
questions.notes: Partial dressing; self-care independence with simple garments.

questions.text: Does your child use a fork?
questions.notes: Fork use for self-feeding; utensil mastery progressing from spoon to fork.
